data.title: Real estate stocks trade sideways as economic sentiment softens
data.description:
Real estate stocks traded sideways during the holiday-shortened week, which saw the markets react to a softening in economic sentiment.

The global bond markets came under pressure on Tuesday, with long-dated yields in the U.S. and UK surging amid renewed tariff uncertainties, rate cut outlook, and other macroeconomic challenges.

However, U.S. Treasury yields fell on Friday after a weaker-than-expected jobs report added to concerns about slowing labor market momentum.

Mortgage rates continued to trend down, increasing optimism in the market. The share of market mortgage applications that were for a refinance reached nearly 47%, the highest since October.

But purchase applications pulled back as slower homebuying activity led to declines in applications across the various loan types.

Over the past six months, economic sentiment has softened significantly as tariff-related fears appear set to trigger job cuts, said a report by S&P Global.

While it remains early, the word "recession" made a comeback after two years during the second-quarter earnings discussions of S&P 500 companies, with 355 mentions, the report noted.

Apollo Global Management's chief economist, Torsten Slok, cautioned that the U.S. housing market is beginning to show signs of cooling.

In a separate report, Slok said labor market may be heading into a softer patch, with early signs pointing to weaker hiring momentum in the months ahead.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) fell 0.34% from the prior week to close at 264.27, while the accompanying Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) declined 0.31% to 42.18.

Meanwhile, REIT indexes delivered mixed returns. Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) gained 0.04% to 2,702.05, while FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs index was unchanged at 779.05.

Next week, certain high-profile conferences, including the Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference; the Bank of America Media, Communications & Entertainment Conference; the Cantor Fitzgerald Global Healthcare Conference; the Citi Global TMT Conference; the Baird Healthcare Conference; and the Wolfe Research TMT Conference, could impact some technology and healthcare stocks.

This in turn could lead to volatility in the related REIT stocks.

Also, the Federal Reserve is set to announce the monetary policy decision on September 17. Traders are now fully pricing in a rate cut in September.

Real estate stocks are likely to see an upward trend next week.

Company News

  • W.P. Carey (WPC) completed additional investments of $250.8M after Q2, bringing its year-to-date investment volume to ~$1.3B and on the verge of attaining its full-year guidance of $1.4B-$1.8B.
  • Iron Mountain (IRM) priced an upsized offering of €1.2 billion aggregate principal amount of its 4.75% senior notes due 2034.
  • Brixmor Property Group (BRX) priced a $400M offering of 4.850% senior notes due 2033.
  • VICI Properties (VICI) declared a $0.45/share quarterly dividend, a 4% increase from the prior dividend of $0.4325.
  • Phillips Edison (PECO) raised its monthly dividend by 5.7% to $0.1083 per share.
  • Other major dividend announcements came from Essex Property Trust (ESS), Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), Sun Communities (SUI), Americold Realty Trust (COLD), Global Medical REIT (GMRE), Centerspace (CSR), Global Self Storage (SELF),
  • Equity Residential (EQR) said its same store revenue growth remains on track to meet the guidance range provided in its second-quarter financial results.
  • SL Green Realty (SLG) has agreed to acquire 346 Madison Avenue and the adjacent site at 11 East 44th Street in New York City for $160.0M.
  • Service Properties Trust (SVC) announced a new 15-year management agreement with Sonesta International for 59 of the REIT's hotels.

Winners & Losers

SBA Communications (SBAC) extended losses from last week, making its way to the top S&P 500 real estate loser position.

Last week, BofA Securities downgraded the telecom tower REIT, considering an expected elevation in tower churn post closing of the ~$23B AT&T and EchoStar deal.

This week, CFO Marc Montagner said at Citi's 2025 global technology, media, and telecommunications conference that the deal could mean disruption in the short term.

The stock lost 3.47% to close the holiday-shortened week at $197.74.

Crown Castle (CCI) (-3.02% W/W to $96.15) and American Tower (AMT) (-2.71% W/W to $198.32) were also notable weekly losers of the sector.

Invitation Homes (INVH) was another notable loser, declining 3.32% from the previous week to close at $30.25.

Among the winners, BXP (BXP) added 5.96% week-over-week to $76.83. 

The office REIT announced an investor day, which will take place on Monday, September 8. The company is set to discuss its near- and long-term strategies; development, redevelopment, acquisition, and disposition activities; and other business and financial matters during the event.

For the broader real estate sector, Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) (+49.44% W/W to $6.65) and Sotherly Hotels (SOHO) (+14.27% W/W to $0.83) were significant gainers.

SITE Centers (SITC) (-23.94% W/W to $9.34) and Ohmyhome (OMH) (-9.60% W/W to $1.13) were the major losers.

Here is a look at the subsector performances:

data.title: From Disney to Google: Why founder names are fading as companies chase verb status
data.description:
The trend of publicly traded companies being named after their founders is a longtime tradition tied to brand identity, personal reputation, and luxury market positioning. However, the practice is slowing as global companies prioritize corporate or aspirational names over the branding of individual founders to support longevity, mergers, and international expansion.

Historically, founder-named companies have added to a sense of personal commitment and heritage. Some classic examples include Ford Motor (F), Disney (NYSE:DIS), Hewlett-Packard (HPE), Charles Schwab (SCHW), Procter & Gamble (PG), Adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY), Marriott International (MAR), Estee Lauder (EL), Anheuser-Busch (BUD), and Johnson & Johnson. The practice was especially common in the apparel and fashion world, where Steven Madden (SHOO), Ralph Lauren (RL), Hugo Boss (OTCPK:BOSSY) (OTC:HUGPF), Versace (OTCPK:PRDSY), Jimmy Choo (CPRI), Chanel, Gucci, Calvin Klein (RL), Prada (OTCPK:PRDSY), Christian Dior (OTCPK:CHDRF) (OTCPK:CHDRY), and Michael Kors (TPR) made their names household brands. Many of those companies took on the branding of both the first and last names of their founders to emphasize a connection to a legacy that could last for decades. 

Earlier in the week, the fashion world lost one of its legends with the passing of Giorgio Armani, who built one of the most influential fashion houses based on the power of his branding.

 

 

 

Looking ahead, the tradition of naming public companies after their founders is expected to become even rarer as companies stretch the envelope in looking for abstract names or searching for a way to have their corporate name be used universally as a verb, such as Google (NASDAQ:GOOG), Uber (UBER), Snapchat (SNAP), and FedEx (FDX). There is also the risk of personal scandals getting in the way of global branding, as was the case with Wynn Resorts (WYNN) and Steve Wynn.

data.title: Insider trades: Nvidia, Bristol Myers Squibb, Gamestop among notable names this week
data.description:
With a shorter trading week, on account of Labour Day on September 1, transactions were muted on the buy side. However, on the sell side, executives from major firms like Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), Medtronic (NYSE:MDT) and GameStop (NYSE:GME) were seen slashing their holdings in the companies. The following is a list of transactions that occurred between September 1 and September 5.

  • Nvidia (NVDA) director Dawn Hudson sold a total of 90,000 shares at prices ranging from $170.63 to $172, for a total value of about $15.38 million. Following the sales, she continues to own 368,887 shares in the chip giant.
  • Bristol Myers Squibb’s (NYSE:BMY) EVP and Chief Financial Officer David Elkins disposed of 56,000 shares at $47.33 each, valued at around $2.65 million, leaving him with 167,379 shares.
  • Jon Moses, director at Take-Two Interactive (NASDAQ:TTWO), sold 1,000 shares at $239.57 each, totaling about $240,000. He now holds 21,901 shares in the firm.
  • At Medtronic (MDT), EVP and President of cardiovascular, Harry Kiil disposed of 8,605 shares at $91.58 apiece, for a transaction worth roughly $788,000, bringing his holdings down to 35,615 shares.
  • David Obstler, chief financial officer at Datadog (NASDAQ:DDOG), sold 12,619 shares at $133.85 each, amounting to $1.69 million, reducing his stake to 386,651 shares.
  • Daniel Moore, principal financial and accounting officer at GameStop (GME), let go of 830 shares for $22.68 apiece, totaling roughly $19,000. Moore continues to hold 119,129 shares of the company.
  • On the buy side, Nick Stanage, director at Huntington Ingalls Industries (NYSE:HII), acquired 3,500 shares at $272.78 each, spending $ 954,730. He now directly owns 3,610 shares.

data.title: Mpox in Africa is no longer a global health emergency, WHO chief says
data.description:
The mpox outbreak that originated from Africa last year can no longer be considered a global emergency due to a sustained decline in cases and improved public health efforts, WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said on Friday.

In a press conference, Dr. Ghebreyesus said that he was lifting the declaration consistent with the recommendation of experts in WHO’s Mpox Emergency Committee, which had convened on Thursday.

In August 2024, the emergency committee of the U.N. agency declared the outbreak a public health emergency of international concern (PHEIC) in a bid to mobilize additional resources and run extensive public awareness campaigns, among other measures.

The classification, WHO’s highest level of warning, came after a newer and deadlier mpox variant emerged in early 2024 from Congo and later spread to neighboring countries, leading the Africa CDC, the region's top public health body, to declare the outbreak a continental emergency.

“I have accepted that advice,” he added, noting that “this decision is based on sustained declines in cases, as in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and in other affected countries, including Burundi, Sierra Leone, and Uganda.”

Dr. Ghebreyesus said that “lifting the emergency declaration does not mean the threat is over, nor that our response will stop,” adding that Africa CDC has decided to stick to its regional emergency declaration during a meeting on Thursday.

Vaccine developers for monkeypox include Bavarian Nordic (OTCPK:BVNKF) (OTCPK:BVNRY) and Emergent BioSolutions (NYSE:EBS).

Developers of therapeutics for monkeypox: GeoVax Labs (GOVX), SIGA Technologies (SIGA), Chimerix (CMRX) and Tonix Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:TNXP).

Monkeypox test makers: Co-Diagnostics (CODX), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF), Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT), Applied DNA Sciences (APDN)

data.title: IREN tops week's financial gainers, Circle Internet falls the most
data.description:
This week's biggest movers among financial stocks included crypto-linked firms, fintechs and housing-related names.

Overall, the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) dipped 1.5% in the past five sessions ended Sept. 5, underperforming the broader S&P 500 Index's 0.3% decrease.

IREN (IREN), a bitcoin (BTC-USD) miner and data center operator, saw its shares jump 14% in the past week, as the price of bitcoin eked out a weekly rise;

Rocket Companies (RKT) and Mr. Cooper (COOP) advanced 13.3% and 13.2%, respectively, after Mr. Cooper shareholders approved the previously announced merger with Rocket.

UWM Holdings (UWMC) accelerated 13.1%; and

PennyMac Financial Services (PFSI) rounded out the five biggest winners with a 7.9% increase.

On the negative side, stablecoin issuer Circle Internet Group (CRCL) gapped down 12.5%;

Payments company Sezzle (SEZL) retreated 10.4%;

Upstart Holdings (UPST) shed 9.3%;

LPL Financial Holdings (LPLA) slid 8.7%; and

Virtu Financial (VIRT) decelerated 8.3%.

data.title: Trump threatens EU with tariff probe after Google fine
data.description:
Hours after the European Commission imposed a €3B ($3.5B) fine on Alphabet’s (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google over the search giant’s advertising practices on Friday, President Donald Trump threatened to launch an investigation that could lead to higher tariffs against the EU.

“Very unfair, and the American Taxpayer will not stand for it! As I have said before, my Administration will NOT allow these discriminatory actions to stand,” Trump said in a social media post.

With the latest fine, the Mountain View, California-based company’s EU liabilities reach $9.5B, far exceeding those imposed on its rival tech giants Apple (APPL), Meta (META), and Microsoft (MSFT), according to an analysis by Bloomberg News.

“They should get their money back! We cannot let this happen to brilliant and unprecedented American Ingenuity,” Trump said, noting that Apple (APPL) also faced a $17B fine “that, in my opinion, should not have been charged.”

His administration will be forced to launch a Section 301 investigation as a response “to nullify the unfair penalties being charged to these Taxpaying American Companies,” he added.

The White House recently launched a Section 301 investigation to impose trade levies on Brazil over the South American nation’s trade practices. A similar probe could lead to retaliatory tariffs against the EU, which only a few weeks ago reached a major trade agreement with the U.S., avoiding trade taxes as high as 30% on certain imports.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Samsara, Fortinet and SailPoint among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the red on Friday after the week saw a cooler than expected jobs report and earnings reports from companies including Broadcom and DocuSign.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) gained 2%, while Dow (DJI) rose 0.7%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Analysts positive on Samsara after Q2 results

Craig-Hallum upgraded Samsara (NYSE:IOT) to Buy from Hold and raised the price target on the shares to $48 from $42 after fiscal second quarter results and guidance exceeded expectations.

Analysts led by Anthony Stoss said they upgraded the shares as the tariff pause is now behind them and the international business is now growing faster than the U.S. The analysts continue to believe Samsara's management is navigating the business well.

Evercore also kept its In Line rating with a $45 price target on Samsara's stock, with analysts led by Kirk Materne saying Samsara delivered a stellar quarter, outpacing Evercore/Street estimates across the board, with total revenue of $392M (+31% year-over-year in constant currency) coming in nicely above guidance.

Wells Fargo maintained its Overweight rating and $50 price target on the shares.

Fortinet downgraded as firewall refresh disappoints

Fortinet (NASDAQ:FTNT) was downgraded to Underweight from Equal-weight by Morgan Stanley due in part to a disappointing firewall refresh.

Morgan Stanley also reduced its PT on the stock to $67 from $78.

"We see FTNT as continuing to be successful with attaching additional products into their installed base, but with the firewall refresh turning out to be smaller than expected, we think FY26/FY27 estimates may need to come down, creating a headwind for the stock," said analysts, led by Meta Marshall.

SailPoint upgraded by MS on identity market strength

Morgan Stanley upgraded SailPoint (NASDAQ:SAIL) to Overweight from Equal-Weight, citing its strength in the identity market.

“SailPoint is a category leader commanding 20%+ market share in the IGA market with a 28% ARR CAGR (from FY23-FY26) primarily supported by the company's ongoing SaaS transition, multi-billion dollar legacy replacement opportunity, and strong upsell/cross-sell motion driving attractive net retention rates,” said Morgan Stanley analyst Meta Marshall and put a PT of $25 on the stock.

Barclays cut its recommendation on office REIT Kilroy Realty (NYSE:KRC) to Equal Weight from Overweight, citing that this could be a "sell the news" moment. "Shares have materially outperformed over the past 6 months, reflecting strengthening leasing momentum in SF (San Francisco) and progress attracting tenants to KOP2 (life science campus in South SF). However, no leases at KOP2 have been announced yet," said analyst Brendan Lynch and maintained PT at $43.

Rosenblatt began coverage on Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) with a Buy rating and a PT of $140 on the stock. Analysts led by Catharine Trebnick said they believe the company sits at a key inflection point where years of investment in market leadership are now set to translate into a durable model of profitable growth and significant free cash flow generation.

Mobileye (NASDAQ:MBLY) was in focus as Deutsche Bank added the Advanced Driver Assistance Systems company to its Catalyst Call Buy List, ahead of IAA Munich next week, where the company will be conducting demo drives of production vehicles equipped with the EyeQ6 High based SuperVision system.

data.title: VinFast follows Tesla to launch in India at competitive prices
data.description:
VinFast Auto (NASDAQ:VFS) made its commercial debut in India on Saturday, offering two SUV models at prices to rival those of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), which launched only a few weeks ago in the world’s third-largest auto market.

The Vietnamese EV maker unveiled its SUV models VF6 and VF7 at an event attended by CEO Pham Sanh Chau in the Indian capital, New Delhi. "India is essential to our mission. Entering India was about understanding their needs, and the response to our entry into the nation was phenomenal," Sanh Chau said.

With its market entry, Hai Phong-based VinFast (NASDAQ:VFS), the first foreign pure-play EV-maker to invest in a production plant in India, is set to compete with domestic rivals such as Mahindra and Mahindra (OTC:MAHMF) as well as global EV makers including Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY).

The ex-showroom price for its cheapest model, the compact SUV VF6, starts from about ₹1.6M ($18.1K), while that for its mid-sized SUV starts from roughly ₹2.1M ($23.0K) during a special introductory period. BYD's (OTCPK:BYDDF) compact SUV Atto 3 is on sale in India at prices starting from ₹2.5M.

In mid-July, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) entered the Indian market with its Model Y electric SUV, priced at around ₹6.0M ($69.8K). However, its launch has fallen short of the Elon Musk-led firm’s own expectations, with orders for just over 600 units received so far, Bloomberg News reported this week, citing people familiar with the matter.

data.title: iPhone Air is likely to be the star of Apple's event: analysts
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) will hold its annual fall product event on Sept. 9, where it is expected to unveil its iPhone 17 line. And while the Pro and Pro Max have been the best-selling iPhone models the last several years, Wall Street is expecting big things from a new model: the “iPhone Air.”

High expectations

Likely slimmer than the base iPhone 17 model, the iPhone Air could breathe new life into the form factor for the Apple-designed smartphone and ultimately drive sales, Citi analyst Atif Malik said. “The most significant change anticipated is on the brand-new iPhone 17 Air model that is Apple’s thinnest and lightest iPhone to date, which will also replace the Plus model,” Malik wrote in a note to clients.

Malik, who has a Buy rating and $245 price target on Apple, said he expects the iPhone 17 family to drive shipments of 82M in 2025, compared to 81M for the iPhone 16 family in 2024.

Looking out further, a number of widely anticipated product launches — an advanced version of Siri, a foldable iPhone and a second-version of the Vision Pro — could drive a stronger replacement cycle, Malik added.

J.P. Morgan analyst Samik Chatterjee echoed those thoughts, noting that the iPhone Air could “appeal to a broader than anticipated consumer demographic,” even if its feature set is closer to the iPhone base models than the more expensive Pro models. 

“While expectations for iPhone Air volumes have moderated in recent months with the feedback from the supply chain that Apple is largely planning for [roughly] 10-15M units in the [second-half] of the calendar year, but there remains room for surprises from better consumer reception,” Chatterjee added.

Pricing and specs

Pricing of the iPhone Air is likely to be paramount to its reception in the marketplace, especially China, where the Tim Cook-led company has run into steep competition from domestic companies, including Huawei, Chatterjee explained.

The analyst said that the price is likely to fall within the range of $899 to $949. However, if Apple were to sell for a price closer to $800, it could “present an opportunity for upside” and imply that more than one iPhone could qualify for subsidies in China, which have helped buoy sales. 

Aside from the pricing, the iPhone Air is expected to live up to its name, and be much lighter than other models at around 145 grams. That's considerably lighter than the iPhone 16 Pro (199 grams), iPhone 16e (167 grams) and iPhone 13 Mini (141 grams). 

The new smartphone is also expected to be slimmer than other iPhones, with its thickness expected to be 5.5 millimeters, or around 2 millimeters thinner than the Pro versions.

That thinness is likely to have some trade-offs, however, with industry experts suggesting it will have a shorter battery life, a single 48MP rear camera (the Pro versions have 3 cameras and the base models have 2) and lack of spatial video support.

Other notable changes for the iPhone Air include a 6.6-inch display (between the Pro and Pro Max); a 120 Hz refresh rate; 12GB of RAM; and Apple's in-house modem, which was first unveiled alongside the iPhone 16e in February.
